case_name: Anatoxin-a control in small well-water system for drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for anatoxin-a contaminated well water intended for drinking water use under low-energy, small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small community well serving a rural drinking water system has detected anatoxin-a after seasonal surface water influence into the aquifer following heavy rain. The utility operates with limited staff, prefers simple and proven treatment steps, and wants to avoid high-pressure membrane systems because of energy and maintenance burden. The raw water is otherwise typical of a low-turbidity groundwater source, so the treatment decision is primarily focused on cyanotoxin control while maintaining reliable drinking water production.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of anatoxin-a for potable use using established small-system treatment approaches and maintain routine drinking water quality.
secondary_goals: use robust treatment suitable for a small utility
avoid unnecessarily complex or high-pressure systems
maintain simple monitoring and operator workload

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited residuals handling capacity; frequent sludge production or difficult concentrate disposal is undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable operation with straightforward seasonal response is required

core_unit_functions.contact_tank: provide mixing and contact time for toxin adsorption
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove PAC particles and any associated solids
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide routine microbiological protection for drinking water

applicability_rationale: anatoxin-a is the clear primary contaminant and activated carbon is a widely recognized cyanotoxin control approach
the raw water has low turbidity and modest DOC, which supports practical adsorption treatment
PAC can be implemented with relatively low energy compared with high-pressure membranes
the process is suitable for small systems when seasonal or episodic toxin events occur
potential_risks: performance depends on PAC dose, contact time, and competitive adsorption from natural organic matter
inadequate filtration can allow carbon carryover
toxin breakthrough can occur if event intensity rises and dosing is not adjusted

core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb dissolved anatoxin-a during fixed-bed contact
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish effluent and retain fines if needed
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished water

applicability_rationale: GAC is an established barrier for many dissolved cyanotoxins and can fit compact small-system treatment layouts
low-turbidity well water is favorable for GAC operation because particulate loading is limited
the process uses low energy and avoids concentrate generation
it can offer simpler routine operation than repeated PAC slurry handling where toxin events are prolonged
potential_risks: media exhaustion requires monitoring and timely replacement or reactivation
breakthrough can be rapid if influent toxin concentration increases unexpectedly
empty bed contact time must be adequate for reliable toxin removal

core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize dissolved anatoxin-a to less harmful compounds
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_or_sand_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and provide polishing

applicability_rationale: oxidation is a recognized cyanotoxin treatment pathway and can be effective for dissolved toxins under controlled conditions
the source water has low turbidity, which supports oxidant contact efficiency
this option can provide rapid response during acute contamination episodes
potential_risks: ozone systems are less attractive for small utilities because of equipment complexity and power demand
oxidation must be validated to ensure adequate anatoxin-a destruction
byproduct formation and bromate risk must be assessed where bromide is present

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_uv_hydrogen_peroxide: generate hydroxyl radicals to degrade anatoxin-a
core_unit_functions.filtration: provide polishing and remove incidental solids

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can technically destroy dissolved cyanotoxins when properly designed
the relatively low DOC and turbidity improve oxidant utilization compared with more colored waters
this route may be considered where direct destruction rather than adsorption is preferred
potential_risks: UV-AOP has higher energy demand than adsorption-based options
chemical handling and process validation increase operator burden
underperforming radical generation can reduce toxin destruction efficiency

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: physically reject dissolved contaminants including small organic molecules
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection in finished water

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is technically capable of reducing dissolved anatoxin-a
it may be relevant only if broader contaminant removal is simultaneously required
potential_risks: high-pressure membranes conflict with the low-energy requirement
concentrate disposal is difficult for a small well-water system
the treatment train is unnecessarily complex for a single cyanotoxin problem in otherwise good-quality well water

preferred_rank_pattern: low_energy small-system compatible adsorption should rank first
high-pressure membrane treatment should not rank first for this case

must_reflect: avoidance of complex high-pressure or high-skill processes unless clearly justified

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for dissolved anatoxin-a control
why low turbidity well water supports adsorption-focused treatment
why ozone_or_aop_and_membrane_options rank lower under small-system low-energy constraints

should_mention: need to distinguish dissolved toxin control from cell removal logic
oxidation byproduct or validation concerns for oxidant-based options
